summary: Stancil argues that dbt Labs’ success rests as much on its community and human-centered approach as its product, but the market tailwinds that protected it are fading. He lays out three futures—stay the course, go on the offensive into adjacent markets, or sell—while noting that the rise of LLM-driven data stacks could reshape what “dbt” needs to be.
money_quotes: Though dbt Labs' success is impressive, this bubbling appreciation from its users is what makes it truly unique.
Building a company at this pace is a marathon of sprints.
Everyone’s friends until we’re hungry, desperate, and there’s blood in the water.
I’m increasingly becoming convinced that the entire modern data stack is on the cusp of getting rebuilt for our AI overlords.
Wall Street doesn't care about how much goodwill dbt Labs has; it doesn’t care about Slack members, or Github stars, or meetups hosted.
key_insight: dbt’s edge has been its human-centered community-driven model, but shifting market dynamics and AI-driven architectures may force it to either expand aggressively or find a strategic exit.
